summary: Fable verifies each suspected Restate SDK replay/concurrency bug against real code + proto + shared-core VM

findings.title: Replayed results are deserialized from raw command protobuf bytes instead of completion notification values

evidence.interpretation: ReplayNextEntryAsync stores the raw COMMAND protobuf payload as the journal entry's Result and marks it Completed. NOTIFICATION messages (which carry the actual value/failure) are routed to HandleIncomingMessage, which only feeds _completions/_signalCompletions TCSes (lines 123-192) and never updates the journal entry. The same pattern exists in StartAsync lines 67-87.

Replay of a completed ctx.Run JSON-deserializes replay.Result, which contains RunCommandMessage protobuf bytes (varint field 11 + string field 12), not the run's value. Identical pattern at RunAsync lines 56-60, RunFutureAsync 216-223, CallAsync 272-276 / 772-776 / 867-871, CallFutureAsync 337-353, AttachInvocationAsync 409-413, GetInvocationOutputAsync 436-441, GetStateAsync 475-479, GetStateKeysAsync 557-561, SendAsync 305-310 / 805-810, GetPromiseAsync 681-685, PeekPromiseAsync 707-711.

replay.Result (protobuf bytes, e.g. first byte 0x58 for RunCommandMessage field 11 or 0x0A for CallCommandMessage field 1) is fed to Utf8JsonReader -> JsonException on every replay of a completed entry, or silently-wrong defaults for empty payloads.

grep -rn WithCompletion across the repo (excluding third_party) matches only this definition — zero call sites. The mechanism intended to merge notification values into journal entries is dead code, confirming notifications never reach the replay read path.

Ground truth: the replayed RunCommandMessage carries NO value — only a completion id and name. The value or terminal failure arrives exclusively in RunCompletionNotificationMessage keyed by completion_id. Same split for Call (lines 409-446), Sleep, GetPromise, AttachInvocation — all completable commands use result_completion_id = 11.

Reference Rust VM: a replayed command is only popped and header-matched against the locally rebuilt command (determinism/dedup check). The result is NEVER read from the command — it is resolved from async_results via NotificationId::CompletionId(result_completion_id), and take_notification (vm/mod.rs:505) returns Value::Success/Failure from the replayed completion notification (enqueued in transitions/input.rs:77-100), so terminal failures re-raise deterministically on replay.

A terminally-failed Run is appended locally as a Completed entry with an empty payload (duplicated at lines 174-184). The report's stated mechanism is slightly off — this local entry is not what replay reads (replay re-reads commands from the wire) — but the stated consequence is correct: on replay the failure lives only in RunCompletionNotificationMessage.failure, which the replay path never consults, so the TerminalException is not re-raised; instead Deserialize<T> on RunCommandMessage bytes throws JsonException (a transient error, causing retry loops) and saga compensation paths that depend on catching TerminalException break.

findings.rootCause: The fork conflates the two halves of the V4 journal model. In the service protocol and the shared-core VM, a replayed COMMAND message is only a determinism checkpoint (header/dedup match in PopJournalEntry/check_entry_header_match); the actual result of every completable operation (Run, Call, Sleep, GetPromise, AttachInvocation, lazy GetState, ...) travels separately in a *CompletionNotificationMessage keyed by result_completion_id, surfaced via async_results/take_notification. The .NET ReplayNextEntryAsync instead stores the raw command protobuf payload as JournalEntry.Result with IsCompleted=true, routes notifications only into CompletionManager TCSes that the blocking replay paths never await, and then JSON-deserializes the command protobuf bytes as the typed result. JournalEntry.WithCompletion — the only mechanism that could merge notification values into the journal — has zero call sites. Net effect: any replay (suspension resume, retry) of a handler containing a completed ctx.Run/ctx.Call/promise throws JsonException or yields garbage, and terminally-failed Runs do not deterministically re-raise TerminalException on replay.
findings.correctBehavior: Per protocol.proto (RunCommandMessage lines 505-508: only result_completion_id=11 + name=12; RunCompletionNotificationMessage lines 512-522: completion_id + oneof {value, failure}; same shape for Call at 409-446) and the Rust VM (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/transitions/journal.rs SysRun: replayed command is popped and header-matched only, then the handle is bound to NotificationId::CompletionId(result_completion_id); src/vm/mod.rs:505 take_notification returns the value/failure from the replayed completion notification enqueued into async_results in transitions/input.rs): on replay the SDK must (1) match the replayed command against what it would have produced, (2) resolve the operation's result exclusively from the completion notification with the matching completion id, (3) surface a Failure notification as a TerminalException, and (4) suspend (not crash) if the notification has not arrived.
findings.fixSpec: Make replay of completable commands resolve through CompletionManager exactly like live execution: (1) In ProtobufCodec add a parser extracting result_completion_id from a replayed command payload (varint field 11 for all completable commands except CompletePromiseCommandMessage which uses field 1 — parse per message type). (2) In InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs ReplayNextEntryAsync (and the StartAsync known-entries loop), for completable command types append JournalEntry.Pending and record the parsed completion id (extend JournalEntry with an int CompletionId, or return it from ReplayNextEntryAsync); keep storing nothing from the command payload as Result. Keep routing notifications through HandleIncomingMessage — it already keys _completions by notification.CompletionId. (3) In every blocking replay branch in Operations.cs (RunSyncReplayAsync, RunAsync x2, RunFutureAsync, CallAsync x3, CallFutureAsync, SendAsync x2, AttachInvocationAsync, GetInvocationOutputAsync, GetStateAsync, GetStateKeysAsync, SleepAsync/SleepFutureAsync, GetPromiseAsync, PeekPromiseAsync) replace `Deserialize<T>(replay.Result)` with: `var tcs = _completions.GetOrRegister(parsedCompletionId); var completion = await tcs.Task; completion.ThrowIfFailure(); return Deserialize<T>(completion.Value);` — continuing to pump ReplayNextEntryAsync/ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync so notifications arriving after the command are delivered, and suspending if none arrives. This restores value-from-notification semantics and deterministic TerminalException re-raise. (4) Delete the dead JournalEntry.WithCompletion or use it to cache completions on entries. Critically, key everything by the completion id parsed from the replayed command payload, NOT by journal index — original execution appends two journal entries per Call (dummy invocation-id slot + pending) while replay appends one per CallCommand, so positional indices diverge.
findings.risks: The fix touches every replay path, so regressions affect all resumed invocations. Two coupled hazards: (a) journal-index divergence — live Call paths append a dummy entry + pending entry (completion id == journal count at write time) while replay appends one entry per wire command, so any index-positional assumption (e.g. CallFutureAsync's `_completions.GetOrRegister(_journal.Count - 1)` fallback, SleepFutureAsync's replayIndex) must be migrated to parsed completion ids in the same change or completions will be keyed inconsistently; (b) ordering/suspension — the completion notification may arrive after the command in the replay stream or never (suspended invocation), so the replay path must keep reading messages while awaiting the TCS and must produce a suspension rather than a deadlock; CancellationToken behavior on indefinite waits needs explicit handling. Also note there is no tests/ directory in the repo, so there is no existing safety net — tests must be added with the fix. The report's sub-claim about lines 128-141 is directionally right but mechanistically imprecise (the locally appended empty entry is not read during replay); the failure-not-re-raised behavior is fixed by (3) above, not by changing that append.
findings.testStrategy: Add unit tests driving InvocationStateMachine over an in-memory duplex pipe with synthetic V4 frames. Replay-success: Start(known_entries=2) + InputCommand + RunCommand(result_completion_id=1,name="x") + RunCompletionNotification(completion_id=1, value=JSON "42") -> ctx.Run replay returns 42 (currently throws JsonException — test fails before fix, passes after). Replay-failure: RunCompletionNotification with failure{code=500} -> replay throws TerminalException(500). Call replay: CallCommand + CallCompletionNotification with JSON body -> typed response; also notification-before-command and command-before-notification orderings. Suspension: command without notification -> replay awaits/suspends, does not crash. Index-divergence: journal with Call (1 wire command) followed by Run, assert the Run resolves against its own completion id. Property/fuzz test: arbitrary protobuf command payloads must never reach JsonSerializer. Integration: e2e against a real restate-server, handler = ctx.Run then ctx.Sleep(long) to force suspension, then assert resume returns the original Run value and a terminally-failing Run re-raises identically after restart.

findings.title: known_entries (commands+notifications) compared against command-only journal Count makes resumed invocations hang in Replaying or silently skip replay

evidence.quote:
// This is the sum of known commands + notifications
  uint32 known_entries = 3;
evidence.interpretation: Ground truth: known_entries counts BOTH commands and notifications, exactly as the report claims.

Rust VM: every received message (command OR notification) increments received_entries toward known_entries; commands are buffered in a VecDeque (NewCommandMessage -> commands.push_back), notifications go to async_results. known_entries is only used to know when the replay STREAM is fully received.
Rust VM: each sys-call during replay pops one buffered COMMAND; Replaying->Processing occurs when the buffered command queue is empty — never by comparing a command count to known_entries.

StartAsync consumes exactly known_entries messages (correct wire framing) but appends ONLY commands to _journal; notifications are handled inline and never counted. So _journal.Count = #commands while KnownEntries = #commands + #notifications.

The replay predicate compares the command-only Count against the combined commands+notifications total. Full-repo grep on KnownEntries confirms no compensation/decrement exists anywhere. With >=1 replayed notification, IsReplaying stays true forever after StartAsync.

Stuck-Replaying consequence: the first ctx.* op enters this loop waiting for a Command from the wire. The runtime already sent all known_entries during StartAsync and only ever sends notifications/acks afterwards (commands flow SDK->runtime), so Count never advances -> infinite wait until abort_timeout, then retry repeats identically. Note also index starts at _journal.Count, so the entries StartAsync buffered are NEVER returned — there is no replay cursor anywhere (grep for '_journal[' / replayIndex confirms), which is what makes the 0-notification case skip replay and re-execute side effects as duplicate new commands.

The incoming pump starts before the handler runs, so when the bug leaves State==Replaying, ReplayNextEntryAsync and ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync additionally race on the same ProtocolReader — but neither ever appends a command, so the hang stands regardless.

evidence.quote: known_entries: 3,
evidence.interpretation: Shared-core's own test for resuming a completed sleep uses known_entries=3 = InputCommand + SleepCommand + SleepCompletionNotification — i.e., the normal resume case always contains >=1 notification, so the .NET hang triggers on every ordinary suspension/resume.

findings.rootCause: Two coupled modeling errors versus the shared-core VM. (1) Accounting: InvocationJournal.IsReplaying compares Count (commands appended to the journal only) against KnownEntries (the StartMessage value, which per protocol.proto:60 is commands + notifications). StartAsync consumes the correct number of wire messages but only commands increment Count, so any replayed journal containing >=1 completion/signal notification (the normal resume case) leaves Count < KnownEntries permanently — State sticks in Replaying with the replay stream already drained, and the first ctx.* operation blocks forever inside ReplayNextEntryAsync's read loop (the runtime never sends Command messages). (2) Missing replay cursor: the Rust VM pops buffered commands one-per-sys-call and exits Replaying when the queue empties; the .NET port buffers all replayed commands into _journal during StartAsync but ReplayNextEntryAsync starts at index = _journal.Count and only returns entries read from the wire AFTER the call, so the buffered entries are never replayed at all. Consequently, in the 0-notification case (Count == KnownEntries) the machine jumps straight to Processing and the handler re-executes every journaled operation as a fresh command (duplicate journal entries / re-executed side effects) — the 'skip replay' arm.
findings.correctBehavior: Per protocol.proto:60-61, known_entries = commands + notifications, and it only delimits how many messages constitute the replayed journal stream. The Rust VM (vm/transitions/input.rs:120-148) counts every received command AND notification toward entries_to_replay while buffering commands into a VecDeque and routing notifications into async_results; once all known_entries messages are received it enters State::Replaying. During Replaying, each sys-call pops exactly one buffered command (vm/transitions/journal.rs:406-408 'commands.pop_front().ok_or(UnavailableEntryError...)') and validates it against the expected command type/payload; the transition Replaying -> Processing happens precisely when the buffered command queue becomes empty (journal.rs:464-475 'if commands.is_empty() { State::Processing { processing_first_entry: true, ... } }'). known_entries is never compared against a command-only counter, and replayed entries are served from the in-memory buffer — never re-read from the wire.
findings.fixSpec: Mirror the Rust VM's buffered-command-queue model over the already-eagerly-buffered journal. (1) InvocationJournal: add 'public int ReplayTarget { get; private set; }' and 'public int ReplayCursor { get; set; }', and a method 'public void CompleteReplayBuffering() { ReplayTarget = Count; ReplayCursor = 1; }' (cursor starts at 1 because entry 0 = Input is consumed by invocation start). Redefine 'IsReplaying => ReplayCursor < ReplayTarget' (the C# analogue of !commands.is_empty()). Remove the Count-vs-KnownEntries comparison; KnownEntries may remain for logging only. (2) InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs StartAsync: after the replay-read for-loop (lines 67-87), call _journal.CompleteReplayBuffering(); keep the existing 'if (!_journal.IsReplaying) State = InvocationState.Processing;'. (3) Rewrite ReplayNextEntryAsync to consume the buffer instead of reading the wire: 'if (_journal.ReplayCursor >= _journal.ReplayTarget) throw new ProtocolException($\"Unavailable replay entry, expected {expectedType}\");' (analogue of UnavailableEntryError), then 'var entry = _journal[_journal.ReplayCursor]; _journal.ReplayCursor++; if (!_journal.IsReplaying) { State = InvocationState.Processing; Log.ReplayCompleted(...); } return entry;' — it becomes synchronous; optionally validate entry.Type against expectedType (analogue of CommandTypeMismatchError). (4) AdvanceReplayIndex: increment ReplayCursor instead of appending an empty entry, with the same Processing transition. (5) Operations.cs replay branches that register completions by journal index (e.g. Sleep at line 383 'var replayIndex = _journal.Count') must use the index of the replayed entry (the cursor value before increment), not Count, so completion ids line up with the replayed command's index. (6) InvocationStateMachine.cs Initialize line 126 can keep 'State = knownEntries > 0 ? Replaying : Processing' since StartAsync immediately corrects it after buffering (known_entries is always >=1 per shared-core KNOWN_ENTRIES_IS_ZERO check).
findings.risks: This rewires the heart of replay, so every Operations.cs replay branch is affected: (a) completion-id correlation — the SDK convention assumes completion id == command index; when switching to cursor-based replay, the registered completion index for replayed Sleep/Call/GetState etc. must be the replayed entry's own index or completions delivered by notification will land on the wrong future; ideally decode result_completion_id from the replayed command payload like the Rust VM does. (b) Fixing ONLY the accounting (e.g., counting commands toward a command-only KnownEntries) without the cursor converts the hang into universal silent skip-replay/re-execution — strictly worse; both halves of the fix must land together. (c) ReplayNextEntryAsync currently double-reads the pipe concurrently with ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync; removing wire reads from replay eliminates that race, but verify nothing else depended on replay-time wire reads (e.g., late notifications are already handled by the pump). (d) AdvanceReplayIndex callers (SetState/ClearState/CompleteAwakeable/SendSignal/CompletePromise) previously appended placeholder entries that kept Count growing; after the change Count semantics during replay shift, so audit anything keying off _journal.Count as 'next command index' while replaying.
findings.testStrategy: Unit tests over an in-memory duplex pipe driving ProtocolReader/Writer, modeled directly on shared-core tests. (1) Hang regression (sleep.rs known_entries=3 analogue): feed Start{known_entries=3} + InputCommand + SleepCommand + SleepCompletionNotification; handler does ctx.Sleep then returns; assert the invocation completes within a short timeout (e.g. 2s), no additional SleepCommand is written to the output stream, and state transitions Replaying->Processing after the sleep is replayed. (2) Skip-replay regression (0 notifications): Start{known_entries=2} + InputCommand + SetStateCommand; handler does ctx.Set with identical key/value; assert NO duplicate SetStateCommand is written and the entry is consumed from the buffer. (3) Fresh invocation (input_output.rs analogue): Start{known_entries=1} + InputCommand; assert State==Processing immediately after StartAsync and normal command emission. (4) Mixed journal (failures.rs known_entries=5 analogue): input + 2 commands + 2 notifications; assert IsReplaying flips false exactly when the last buffered command is consumed, not before/after. (5) UnavailableEntry: journal exhausted but handler issues an extra replayed-state op — assert ProtocolException rather than wire read. Run all with a watchdog timeout to catch any reintroduced read-loop hang.

findings.title: Concurrent PipeReader reads: ReplayNextEntryAsync races the ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync pump whenever the replay batch contains a notification

evidence.interpretation: The message pump starts immediately after StartAsync and before the handler runs. The async pump executes synchronously up to its first pending _reader.ReadAsync, so a read is already registered on the PipeReader when the handler begins.
Reader #1: the pump loops on _reader regardless of Replaying/Processing state — there is no gate preventing it from reading while replay is still in progress.

Reader #2: the replay path performs fresh wire reads on the same ProtocolReader (the report's ~207 line number is exact: line 207). It is invoked from the handler task while the pump's read is pending.

StartAsync consumes exactly KnownEntries messages (counting notifications, matching the wire batch) but appends only commands to the journal. If k>=1 notifications are in the batch, journal.Count = KnownEntries - k, so IsReplaying stays true and State remains Replaying when the pump starts.

evidence.quote:
public int Count { get; private set; }

public bool IsReplaying => Count < KnownEntries;
evidence.interpretation: Replay-done accounting compares command-only Count against KnownEntries which (per ground truth) counts commands AND notifications — the mismatch that keeps the replay path alive after the wire batch is fully consumed.
Every journaled operation (GetState, Run, Call, Sleep, promises — 20+ call sites) takes the wire-reading replay path while State == Replaying, i.e., concurrently with the running pump.

No lock or single-reader enforcement; shared mutable decoder state (_state/_pendingHeader) plus a PipeReader whose contract forbids concurrent ReadAsync (InvalidOperationException 'Concurrent reads or writes are not supported'). The fork's own comment at InvocationHandler.cs:113-115 acknowledges this exception class.

GROUND TRUTH: in the official VM, notifications received during the replay window count toward known_entries (PostReceiveEntry is reached from both NewNotificationMessage and NewCommandMessage), and commands are buffered into an in-memory queue before the handler progresses.

GROUND TRUTH: replay consumes ONLY from the buffered commands queue (pop_front); a missing entry is UnavailableEntryError, never a fresh wire read. Replaying->Processing happens when the buffer empties. The VM is sans-IO with a single input-consumption point — concurrent wire readers are impossible by construction.

evidence.quote:
StartMessage { ... known_entries: 3, ... }
.input(input_entry_message(b"Till"))
.input(GetLazyStateCommandMessage { key: ..., result_completion_id: 1, ... })
.input(GetLazyStateCompletionNotificationMessage { completion_id: 1, ... })
evidence.interpretation: GROUND TRUTH wire shape: known_entries=3 includes a completion notification. Feeding this exact standard batch to the .NET port leaves journal.Count=2 < KnownEntries=3 after StartAsync, State stays Replaying, and the handler's GetState then races the pump on the PipeReader.

findings.rootCause: Two readers share one ProtocolReader/PipeReader with no single-reader ownership: (1) the pump ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync is started unconditionally right after StartAsync (InvocationHandler.cs:50) and immediately parks a pending ReadAsync; (2) ReplayNextEntryAsync (Protocol.cs:207) performs fresh wire reads from the handler task whenever State == Replaying. The reachable trigger is a replay-accounting mismatch: StartAsync consumes exactly KnownEntries messages (notifications included, matching the runtime's batch semantics) but InvocationJournal.IsReplaying compares command-only Count against KnownEntries, which per the Rust VM counts commands AND notifications. So any replay batch containing >=1 completion notification (the routine shape for a resumed invocation with a completed call/sleep/state-get, per shared-core test state.rs entry_already_completed) leaves State == Replaying after StartAsync. The handler's first journaled operation then calls _reader.ReadMessageAsync concurrently with the pump's pending read — deterministically violating the PipeReader contract (InvalidOperationException 'Concurrent reads or writes are not supported') and corrupting ProtocolReader's shared _state/_pendingHeader decoder state; even if reads serialize by luck, the pump silently drops command messages (HandleIncomingMessage ignores commands) and nothing further is on the wire anyway, so ReplayNextEntryAsync hangs forever. Outcome: resumed invocations crash with a 500/retry loop or hang until timeout.
findings.correctBehavior: The shared-core VM is sans-IO with exactly one input-consumption point. During WaitingReplayEntries it buffers all known_entries messages — commands into a VecDeque, notifications into async_results — counting BOTH toward known_entries (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/transitions/input.rs:79-148, PostReceiveEntry: 'received_entries += 1; if ... entries_to_replay == received_entries { State::Replaying { commands, ... } }'). Replay then consumes exclusively from the buffered queue: 'commands.pop_front().ok_or(UnavailableEntryError...)' (vm/transitions/journal.rs:406-408), and transitions Replaying->Processing exactly when 'commands.is_empty()' (journal.rs:464-476). Replay never reads the wire; a missing entry is a protocol error (UnavailableEntryError), not a blocking read. Test state.rs:47-70 confirms known_entries=3 includes the completion notification. Therefore after the .NET StartAsync has consumed the full batch, replay must be driven solely by the in-memory journal, and the pump must be the sole wire reader.
findings.fixSpec: Enforce single-reader ownership by making replay consume only the journal buffered in StartAsync, mirroring Rust's Replaying{commands} pop_front: (1) InvocationJournal.cs — add a replay cursor and seal step: 'public int ReplayCommandCount { get; private set; }', 'public int ReplayCursor { get; private set; } = 1;' (entry 0 = Input), 'public void SealReplay() => ReplayCommandCount = Count;', change 'IsReplaying => ReplayCursor < ReplayCommandCount', and add 'public JournalEntry TakeNextReplayEntry() { if (!IsReplaying) throw new ProtocolException("Unavailable entry during replay"); return this[ReplayCursor++]; }'. (2) Protocol.cs StartAsync — after the known-entries loop call '_journal.SealReplay();' before the IsReplaying check (this also fixes the accounting: replay-done is now defined by buffered commands consumed, not KnownEntries-with-notifications). (3) Protocol.cs — replace ReplayNextEntryAsync's wire loop with a synchronous buffer pop that never touches _reader: 'var entry = _journal.TakeNextReplayEntry(); if (entry.Type != expectedType) throw new ProtocolException($"Command type mismatch: expected {expectedType}, got {entry.Type}"); if (!_journal.IsReplaying) { State = InvocationState.Processing; Log.ReplayCompleted(...); } return entry;' (mirrors CommandTypeMismatchError/UnavailableEntryError in journal.rs). (4) Protocol.cs AdvanceReplayIndex — advance the cursor via TakeNextReplayEntry instead of appending an empty entry. (5) Operations.cs — the ~20 'await ReplayNextEntryAsync(...)' call sites become calls to the sync method (keep an async-shaped wrapper if churn must be minimized). After this change, after StartAsync returns the ONLY code path calling _reader.ReadMessageAsync is ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync. Note: wiring replayed lazy-command RESULTS to operations (replay.Result currently holds raw command protobuf bytes, and lazy results should come from _completions populated by the batch notifications) is a separate correctness defect outside B3's scope, but the buffer-pop structure above is the prerequisite for fixing it the shared-core way.
findings.risks: 1) Entangled latent bug: the replay branches deserialize 'replay.Result', which after this fix is the buffered raw command protobuf payload (e.g., GetLazyStateCommandMessage bytes), not the operation result — replayed lazy operations need their results from _completions (notifications buffered during StartAsync), so replay output correctness requires a follow-up fix; B3's fix removes the crash/hang and the contract violation but may surface that defect as deserialization errors in tests. 2) Sealing replay by buffered-command count changes IsReplaying semantics for any code reading InvocationStateMachine.IsReplaying (public Context.IsReplaying surface). 3) AdvanceReplayIndex currently appends empty entries, shifting later completion-id arithmetic ((uint)_journal.Count); switching to a cursor must keep Count growth identical or audit completion-id derivation. 4) Kestrel's HTTP/2 request-body PipeReader behavior on concurrent reads is implementation-defined (may corrupt rather than throw), so reproduction symptoms vary by transport — the fix must be validated against both Pipe-based tests and a real Kestrel host.
findings.testStrategy: Unit/integration tests over an in-memory Pipe driving InvocationHandler.HandleAsync, mirroring shared-core wire shapes: (a) port of state.rs entry_already_completed — Start(known_entries=3) + InputCommand + GetLazyStateCommand + GetLazyStateCompletionNotification, handler calls GetState then returns; assert no InvalidOperationException ('Concurrent reads or writes are not supported'), no hang (bounded timeout), and an OutputCommand+End is produced; (b) same shape for Sleep and Call completions inside the batch; (c) pure-command batch (k=0) regression to confirm State->Processing unchanged; (d) single-reader invariant test: wrap ProtocolReader with an Interlocked pending-read counter asserting it never exceeds 1 across all replay scenarios; (e) fuzz the replay batch composition (random interleavings of commands/notifications within known_entries) asserting the invariant and termination. Pre-fix, test (a) must fail deterministically (exception or timeout) to prove the repro; post-fix all pass.

findings.title: Awakeable signal index starts at 0 instead of 17, colliding with reserved built-in signal range (CANCEL = 1)

evidence.quote: private int _nextSignalIndex;
evidence.interpretation: Field is default-initialized to 0; no other assignment exists anywhere (grep over src/ shows only this declaration and the increment in Operations.cs). The first awakeable therefore gets signal index 0, the second gets 1.
Awakeable() allocates the raw counter value as the signal index and bakes it into the externally visible awakeable ID.

Same wire format as shared-core's awakeable_id_str (vm/mod.rs:1355-1362: AWAKEABLE_PREFIX = "sign_1" + base64url(invocation_id || put_u32(signal_id))), so the index value is semantically meaningful to the runtime: resolving this ID delivers a SignalNotification with exactly this idx to the invocation.

Ground truth: the reference Rust VM starts user signal indices at 17 because 1-16 are reserved for built-in signals. sys_awakeable (vm/mod.rs:872-886) draws from this counter via next_signal_notification_id() (context.rs:59-63) to build the awakeable id.

evidence.quote: pub(crate) const CANCEL_SIGNAL_ID: u32 = 1;
evidence.interpretation: Signal index 1 is the built-in CANCEL signal. The proto confirms: protocol.proto:668-672 'enum BuiltInSignal { UNKNOWN = 0; CANCEL = 1; reserved 2 to 15; }'.
The fork itself uses Idx=1 as CANCEL when sending, proving the reserved meaning of index 1 is acknowledged elsewhere in the same codebase.

Incoming SignalNotifications are dispatched purely by Idx into the same _signalCompletions map used by awakeables. A runtime-sent CANCEL signal (Idx=1, Void result) would spuriously complete the second awakeable as Success(empty), and an external resolve of the second awakeable's ID (encoding idx 1) is indistinguishable from a cancellation signal.

findings.rootCause: The fork's per-invocation signal index counter (_nextSignalIndex, InvocationStateMachine.cs:31) is left at its C# default of 0 and incremented per awakeable (Operations.cs:618), so awakeables receive signal indices 0, 1, 2, ... The service protocol reserves signal indices 1-16 for built-in signals (CANCEL = 1; proto reserves 2-15), and the reference shared-core VM therefore starts its counter at 17 (context.rs:109, comment "1 to 16 are reserved!"). Because the awakeable ID encodes the index ("sign_1" + base64url(rawInvocationId || BE32(idx))) and incoming SignalNotifications are dispatched by raw Idx, the SECOND awakeable created in any invocation occupies index 1 and aliases the CANCEL signal in both directions: a runtime cancellation spuriously resolves that awakeable with an empty success payload, and resolving that awakeable's ID is wire-identical to signaling CANCEL on the invocation. Index 0 (first awakeable) is also out of spec (BuiltInSignal.UNKNOWN = 0; shared-core never emits signal ids below 17).
findings.correctBehavior: Shared-core VM, third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/context.rs:101-114: Journal::default() initializes "signal_index: 17" with the comment "// 1 to 16 are reserved!", and sys_awakeable (vm/mod.rs:872-886) builds the awakeable id from next_signal_notification_id(), so the first user awakeable gets signal id 17, the second 18, etc. CANCEL_SIGNAL_ID = 1 (service_protocol/mod.rs:31); protocol.proto:668-672 defines BuiltInSignal { UNKNOWN = 0; CANCEL = 1; reserved 2 to 15; }. User-allocated signal indices must never fall in 0-16.
findings.fixSpec: In /home/steve/git/github.com/stevefan1999-personal/restate-sdk-dotnet/src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/StateMachine/InvocationStateMachine.cs line 31, initialize the counter at the first non-reserved index, mirroring shared-core context.rs:109. Minimal change: replace "private int _nextSignalIndex;" with a named constant plus initializer, e.g. "// Signal indices 1-16 are reserved for built-in signals (CANCEL = 1); user signals start at 17, matching sdk-shared-core vm/context.rs. private const int FirstUserSignalIndex = 17; private int _nextSignalIndex = FirstUserSignalIndex;". No change needed in Operations.cs (the post-increment allocation then yields 17, 18, ...). Optionally also tie ProtobufCodec.cs:382's magic "Idx = 1" to a shared CancelSignalIndex constant, but that is cosmetic and not required for correctness.
findings.risks: 1) Compatibility with in-flight invocations created by the buggy build: previously issued awakeable IDs encode indices 0/1/..., and journaled SignalNotifications carry those low indices; after the fix a replay re-allocates indices from 17, so old suspended invocations holding pre-fix awakeable IDs will never match — acceptable on a pre-release fork branch but worth noting in the changelog. 2) The existing test Awakeable_InProcessingMode_ReturnsIdAndTcs (InvocationStateMachineTests.cs:226-237) only asserts the "sign_1" prefix, so it keeps passing; do not rely on it as regression coverage. 3) The Protocol.cs dispatch still routes a genuine CANCEL signal (Idx=1) into _signalCompletions where no TCS will be registered post-fix (TryComplete on index 1 becomes a no-op) — the fork apparently has no cancellation-signal handling yet; that is a separate, pre-existing gap, not regressed by this fix.
findings.testStrategy: Unit tests in InvocationStateMachineTests.cs: (a) create two awakeables on a fresh state machine, strip the "sign_1" prefix, Base64Url-decode, and assert the trailing BE32 of the first ID is 17 and the second is 18 (mirrors shared-core's expected ids; also asserts no index falls in 0-16); (b) feed a SignalNotification message with Idx = 1 (Void result, the runtime's CANCEL shape) into the state machine after creating two awakeables and assert neither awakeable TCS completes; (c) feed SignalNotification Idx = 17 with a Value payload and assert the first awakeable's TCS completes with that payload. Optionally an integration test against a real restate-server: create 2+ awakeables, resolve the second via the ingress /restate/awakeables/{id}/resolve endpoint, and assert the handler receives the payload and the invocation is not cancelled.

findings.title: Parallel ctx.RunAsync fan-out races on unsynchronized shared state and journals Run entries in completion order, silently cross-wiring results on replay

evidence.interpretation: RunFutureAsync is started but NOT awaited; the incomplete ValueTask is stored in LazyRunFuture. Its continuation will resume on a thread-pool thread when the user action completes, concurrently with the handler and with other in-flight RunAsync continuations.

The RunCommand journal entry is created AFTER the action completes (completion order), unlike CallFutureAsync (line 364) and SleepFutureAsync (line 393) which Append a Pending entry synchronously at creation. Everything in this continuation (Serialize, _journal.Count reads, ProtocolWriter writes, Flush) runs unsynchronized on the thread pool.

Completion id is a racy read of _journal.Count, read twice per run and only Append-ed after flush. Two concurrent run completions read the same Count (duplicate completion ids); an interleaved Append between WriteRunCommand and WriteRunProposal tears the command/proposal id pair.

The shared serialize buffer and reusable Utf8JsonWriter explicitly assume no concurrent access, but RunFutureAsync continuations violate that assumption: GetJsonWriter() calls _serializeBuffer.ResetWrittenCount(), so a concurrent Serialize corrupts or invalidates another continuation's WrittenMemory before it is copied/written.

Header and payload are written in two non-atomic steps on a shared PipeWriter with no lock anywhere in the SDK (only lock found is in unrelated ServiceDefinitionRegistry). Concurrent WriteMessage/FlushAsync interleaves frame bytes and corrupts the protocol stream; PipeWriter is not thread-safe.

Replay matching is purely positional: the expectedType parameter is never read in the method body, and name is merely stamped onto the appended entry, never compared. If first-attempt completion order differed from code (creation) order, replay silently hands run A the result journaled for run B.

Ground truth: the reference VM journals the RunCommand and allocates result_completion_id at sys_run CREATION time, before the closure runs; closure execution is queued separately. Journal command order is therefore deterministic creation order regardless of completion order.
Run results are attached by result_completion_id (allocated at command creation), not by position; the protocol is designed so proposals may arrive in any completion order after the RunCommand.

Ground truth: the reference VM validates each replayed command against the expected command (type and header, including name) and raises CommandMismatchError on divergence — never silently returns a mismatched entry. The VM trait also takes &mut self, structurally serializing all access; the fork has no equivalent mutual exclusion.

findings.rootCause: RunFutureAsync inverts the shared-core two-phase Run protocol: instead of journaling the RunCommand (and fixing its completion id) synchronously at future-creation time like the Rust VM's SysRun, it defers the entire journal/wire interaction into the continuation that fires after the user action completes. Because DefaultContext.RunAsync does not await that ValueTask, those continuations run on arbitrary thread-pool threads with no mutual exclusion around the shared _serializeBuffer/_jsonWriter (whose comment wrongly assumes single-threaded access), the unsynchronized InvocationJournal (Count++/array-grow Append, double read of Count for completion ids), and the unsynchronized ProtocolWriter/PipeWriter (interleaved frames). Independently, journal order for Run entries becomes completion order — nondeterministic across attempts — while ReplayNextEntryAsync matches entries purely positionally and ignores its expectedType/name parameters, so a different completion order on a previous attempt silently cross-wires Run results (and can even hand a Run a Call entry) instead of failing with a journal-mismatch error.
findings.correctBehavior: Per shared-core v0.10.0: sys_run journals RunCommandMessage{name, result_completion_id} immediately at call time via SysSimpleCompletableEntry, with result_completion_id = journal.next_completion_notification_id() (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/transitions/journal.rs:659-713) — journal command order is fixed by creation order, never by closure completion order. The closure result is later attached via ProposeRunCompletionMessage keyed by result_completion_id (protocol.proto:138-144), so proposals may arrive in any order. On replay, every command is validated against the expected command via check_entry_header_match → CommandMismatchError (journal.rs:887-902), and pending runs found without a stored completion are re-enqueued for execution (journal.rs:695-710). All VM access is &mut self — concurrent mutation is structurally impossible; SDK adapters must serialize access externally.
findings.fixSpec: Minimal fix in three parts, mirroring shared-core: (1) In InvocationStateMachine.Operations.cs RunFutureAsync, move journal-entry creation to the synchronous prefix BEFORE `await action()`: capture `completionId = (uint)_journal.Count`, `entryIndex = _journal.Append(JournalEntry.Pending(JournalEntryType.Run, name))`, `tcs = _completions.GetOrRegister(entryIndex)`, write RunCommand with that completionId and flush — all before the first await, so journal/wire order equals creation order (matching SysRun). After the action completes, serialize and write ProposeRunCompletion using the CAPTURED completionId (not a fresh _journal.Count read), flush, mark the entry completed, and SetResult on the tcs. (2) Because the post-action segment still runs on the thread pool, guard every mutate-write-flush section (Serialize via _serializeBuffer + WriteCommand + FlushAsync + journal mutation) with a single async mutex (SemaphoreSlim(1,1) field on InvocationStateMachine) — this is the .NET analogue of the &mut self/mutex discipline shared-core requires; serialization into _serializeBuffer must happen inside the lock. (3) In InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs ReplayNextEntryAsync, actually use the parameters: after fetching replayEntry, throw ProtocolException($"Journal mismatch at index {index}: expected {expectedType} '{name}', found {replayEntry.Type}") when replayEntry.Type != expectedType (the .NET analogue of CommandMismatchError), optionally also comparing the name parsed from the replayed RunCommand payload. Note the replay path for a pending Run (command journaled, no completion) should re-execute the action and propose the completion, per journal.rs:695-710 — at minimum throw a clear error rather than deserializing garbage.
findings.risks: Moving the RunCommand write before action execution changes RunFutureAsync's return shape/timing: DefaultContext.RunAsync's IsCompletedSuccessfully fast-path and LazyRunFuture must still resolve the value from the tcs/serialized result — keep the (Tcs, Result) tuple but ensure the tcs is registered eagerly. Adding a semaphore introduces a small per-operation cost and potential deadlock if FlushAsync is held across a slow pipe — keep the critical section to serialize+write+flush only. Replay-type validation may surface latent mismatches previously masked (e.g., the existing replay path stores the raw RunCommandMessage protobuf bytes as the entry Result and deserializes them as JSON — a separate latent bug in Run replay result delivery that this fix will expose and which likely needs the completion-notification path used instead). Blocking RunSync and the eager RunAsync overloads also flush-then-Append non-atomically (RunSyncAwaitFlush appends after an await) and should be reviewed under the same lock. The pending-Run re-execution semantics (journal.rs:695-710) are unimplemented; the minimal fix only fails loudly there, full parity needs follow-up.
findings.testStrategy: Unit tests over an in-memory duplex pipe (ProtocolReader/Writer): (1) Ordering: create ctx.RunAsync("A") and ctx.RunAsync("B") whose actions are gated by TaskCompletionSources; complete B first, then A; parse the emitted frames and assert RunCommand("A") precedes RunCommand("B") with sequential distinct result_completion_ids, and that ProposeRunCompletion messages reference the matching ids regardless of completion order (fails today). (2) Replay cross-wiring: feed a journal whose Run entries were recorded in reversed order and assert the SDK now throws a journal-mismatch ProtocolException (or, with name validation, the correct re-pairing) instead of silently returning swapped values; also assert ReplayNextEntryAsync throws when the positional entry type differs from expectedType (e.g., Call entry where Run expected). (3) Concurrency stress/fuzz: launch N (e.g., 64) RunAsync futures completing simultaneously via a released barrier, read the full output stream, and assert every frame parses with valid headers, monotonically consistent ids, and journal Count == expected (today: corrupted frames, duplicate ids, lost Appends). Run under the thread-pool with ThreadPool.SetMinThreads raised to force genuine parallelism; repeat 100x for flake detection.

findings.title: ctx.Send blocks awaiting the CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification instead of being fire-and-forget; replay path decodes command protobuf as the invocation id

evidence.interpretation: First SendAsync overload (object-based, line 300): after flushing the OneWayCallCommand it awaits tcs.Task, which is only completed when the runtime delivers a CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification. The one-way send therefore blocks user code on a runtime round trip.

Second SendAsync overload (generic TRequest, line 799) has the identical blocking await. Both public IContext.Send paths (DefaultContext.cs:145-154, 193-196) route through these, so every ctx.Send stalls until the notification arrives.

On replay, replay.Result is the raw detached OneWayCallCommandMessage protobuf payload (Protocol.cs:212-215 appends JournalEntry.Completed with the command payload). UTF-8-decoding it yields protobuf garbage (or "" for an empty command), not the invocation id — confirming the report's secondary claim about a bogus/empty handle id on replay.

The TCS awaited by SendAsync is only ever completed by an incoming CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification routed by completion_id. There is no local short-circuit, proving the await is a genuine runtime round-trip dependency (and a suspension/hang in any mode where notifications are not delivered inline).

Ground truth: the reference VM's sys_send is fully synchronous — it enqueues the OneWayCallCommandMessage and immediately returns a SendHandle wrapping the notification handle. The invocation id is materialized lazily, only if the SDK later calls take_notification (lib.rs:307) on that handle (Rust SDK: send_handle.invocation_id().await). The VM never blocks a send on the notification.

The proto defines the invocation-id notification as the sole, asynchronous completion of a one-way call. Nothing in the protocol requires the SDK to wait for CallInvocationIdCompletionNotificationMessage (proto lines 428-436) before continuing user code.

evidence.quote: public readonly record struct InvocationHandle(string InvocationId);
evidence.interpretation: The public handle eagerly carries the resolved id string, which is what structurally forces the blocking await — there is no lazy resolution surface analogous to Rust's SendHandle.

findings.rootCause: The fork models ctx.Send as a request/response operation: it registers a TaskCompletionSource for the invocation-id notification slot and awaits it before returning, because the public InvocationHandle type (readonly record struct with an eager string InvocationId) demands the id be resolved up front. The shared-core reference (sys_send, vm/mod.rs:796-859) instead returns a SendHandle immediately and resolves the invocation id lazily via take_notification only when requested. Additionally, the replay branch misreads the journal: ReplayNextEntryAsync stores the raw OneWayCallCommandMessage protobuf payload as the entry Result (Protocol.cs:212-215), and SendAsync UTF-8-decodes that command payload as if it were the invocation id, instead of sourcing the id from the replayed CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification at invocation_id_notification_idx (compounding the B1 completion-id/journal-index conflation).
findings.correctBehavior: Per the shared-core VM, a one-way send must return without waiting: sys_send (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/mod.rs:796-859) enqueues the OneWayCallCommandMessage and immediately returns SendHandle { invocation_id_notification_handle }; the id is only obtained later via take_notification(handle) (lib.rs:307) if the user explicitly asks. The proto (protocol.proto:452-477) marks OneWayCallCommandMessage "Completable: Yes (only one notification with invocation id)" — i.e., CallInvocationIdCompletionNotificationMessage (proto:428-436) arrives asynchronously and on replay is re-delivered as a journal notification keyed by completion_id; it is never read out of the command payload.
findings.fixSpec: 1) In both SendAsync overloads (Operations.cs ~300-328 and ~799-827): keep appending the OneWayCall journal entry and registering the TCS at the notification index, but return immediately after FlushAsync — delete the `await tcs.Task` / id-decoding lines. 2) Make the invocation id lazy: change InvocationHandle (src/Restate.Sdk/InvocationHandle.cs) to wrap the pending Task<CompletionResult> and expose `ValueTask<string> GetInvocationIdAsync()` (resolving completion.StringValue), mirroring Rust's SendHandle.invocation_id(); retain a constructor from a known string (completed task) for ingress/client call sites. 3) Fix the replay branch: instead of `Encoding.UTF8.GetString(replay.Result.Span)`, parse the replayed command payload with Gen.OneWayCallCommandMessage.Parser to recover InvocationIdNotificationIdx, register `_completions.GetOrRegister((int)idx)`, and wrap that TCS in the lazy handle — the replayed CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification (handled inline by ReplayNextEntryAsync/HandleIncomingMessage via completion_id routing) will complete it. Update DefaultContext.Send overloads only if the InvocationHandle constructor signature changes.
findings.risks: Changing InvocationHandle from `record struct(string InvocationId)` to a lazy handle is a public API break — audit other constructors of InvocationHandle (RestateEndpointRouteBuilderExtensions.cs, RestateServiceCollectionExtensions.cs, InvocationHandler.cs) which build it from already-known ingress ids and must use the eager constructor. The lazy TCS relies on the read loop continuing to pump notifications after the handler returns from Send — verify the processing-phase reader keeps consuming messages, otherwise GetInvocationIdAsync deadlocks. The fix interacts with B1: this fork routes notifications by completion_id == journal index (entryIndex == invocationIdNotificationIdx here only by coincidence of consecutive appends); if B1's fix changes the completion-id scheme, the registration index in both send paths must follow it. The replay fix adds a protobuf parse of the stored command payload, which assumes Protocol.cs continues storing the raw payload in JournalEntry.Result.
findings.testStrategy: Unit tests against the state machine with a scripted duplex stream: (a) non-replay: invoke SendAsync, assert it completes without any CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification being delivered (fire-and-forget), and that the OneWayCallCommandMessage was flushed with the right invocation_id_notification_idx; then deliver the notification and assert GetInvocationIdAsync resolves to the id. (b) Stall regression: with the old code shape, a withheld notification hangs Send — assert new code returns within the flush. (c) Replay: feed journal [StartMessage, InputCommand, OneWayCallCommand(payload with idx N), CallInvocationIdCompletionNotification(completion_id=N, invocation_id=\"inv_123\")] and assert the returned handle resolves \"inv_123\" rather than UTF-8 garbage from the command payload. (d) End-to-end sample against a real Restate runtime verifying ctx.Send latency does not include a notification round trip and that handle ids survive a forced replay (kill/restore).

findings.title: _initialState cache conflates "have eager state" with "eager state is complete": partial-state Gets silently return default after SetState, and replay never repopulates the cache

evidence.interpretation: Whenever the dictionary is non-null, a missing key returns default with no GetLazyStateCommand. This is only valid if the cache is COMPLETE; there is no partial-state flag anywhere. Note also this check precedes the State == Replaying check at line 475.

With StartMessage.partial_state=true the eager map is left null (the PartialState bit and any partial state_map entries are discarded); InvocationStateMachine.cs:124 then sets _initialState = null. 'Partial' is encoded only as null-ness.

Two confirmed defects in one method: (1) `_initialState ??= new ...` flips a partial-state invocation into 'complete cache' mode containing only the written key, so any subsequent GetStateAsync of another key hits the line-472 `return default` instead of issuing GetLazyStateCommand — silent wrong data. (2) The Replaying branch returns early WITHOUT updating the cache, so first-run and replay produce different `_initialState` contents; under partial state a post-Set Get takes the eager path on first run (no journal entry written) but the replay path on retry (consuming a journal entry that does not exist) → command mismatch or non-deterministic divergence.

Ground truth: the shared-core models an explicit is_partial flag (default true) and a tri-state lookup — absent key under partial state is Unknown, NOT Empty. Cleared keys are kept as None markers (clear() inserts None; clear_all() sets is_partial=false). The .NET fork models none of this.

sys_state_set/sys_state_clear/sys_state_clear_all mutate eager_state UNCONDITIONALLY, before do_transition — i.e. during replay too. The .NET SetState/ClearState/ClearAllState replay branches (Operations.cs:504-507, 524-528, 541-545) skip the cache entirely, confirming the report's replay-divergence claim.

Ground truth for SysStateGet in Processing: Unknown → send GetLazyStateCommand; Empty/Value → journal AND send a GetEagerStateCommandMessage with the observed result inline. In State::Replaying the eager cache is never consulted — the journaled GetEagerState/GetLazyState command is popped and its recorded result used. The .NET eager-hit path journals nothing and sends nothing, which is what makes its first-run/replay code paths able to diverge at all.

findings.rootCause: The fork collapses the shared-core's EagerState { is_partial: bool, values: HashMap<String, Option<Bytes>> } into a single nullable Dictionary (`_initialState`, InvocationStateMachine.cs:38), using null-ness to mean "partial". This invariant is broken the moment SetState executes under partial state: `_initialState ??= new Dictionary<...>(4)` (Operations.cs:516) materializes the dictionary, and GetStateAsync's complete-cache short-circuit (Operations.cs:468-473) then treats every absent key as known-empty, returning default instead of issuing GetLazyStateCommand. Compounding it, the Replaying branches of SetState/ClearState/ClearAllState (Operations.cs:504-507/524-528/541-545) skip the cache mutation that the Rust VM performs unconditionally (mod.rs:603/627/650), and eager hits never journal a GetEagerStateCommand (unlike transitions/journal.rs:325-345), so the eager-vs-lazy decision and the observed values are not deterministic across first run and replay.
findings.correctBehavior: Per protocol.proto (StartMessage field 5 `bool partial_state = 5`) the runtime may send a truncated state_map. The shared-core VM (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/context.rs:373-435) therefore keeps `is_partial` (default TRUE) plus a map where None = known-cleared; EagerState::get returns Unknown for absent keys under partial state and Empty only when complete. SysStateGet (transitions/journal.rs:291-356) maps Unknown → GetLazyStateCommandMessage (real runtime roundtrip), and Empty/Value → a journaled+sent GetEagerStateCommandMessage carrying the result inline, with the Replaying branch consuming that recorded entry rather than the cache. sys_state_set/clear/clear_all (mod.rs:603, 627, 650) mutate the eager cache unconditionally — including during replay — and clear_all flips is_partial=false.
findings.fixSpec: Mirror the shared-core EagerState model. (1) ProtocolTypes.cs/ProtobufCodec.ParseStartMessage: always materialize state_map into the dictionary and add a `bool PartialState` field to StartMessageFields (currently discarded). (2) InvocationStateMachine.cs: replace `Dictionary<string, ReadOnlyMemory<byte>>? _initialState` with a never-null `Dictionary<string, ReadOnlyMemory<byte>?> _eagerState` (null value = known-cleared) plus `bool _eagerStateIsPartial` (true when partial_state or before Initialize, matching EagerState::default). (3) Operations.cs GetStateAsync (Processing path): tri-state lookup — key present with value → return it; present with null → default; absent → default only if !_eagerStateIsPartial, otherwise fall through to the existing GetLazyStateCommand path. On an eager resolution, append a completed GetState journal entry and write a GetEagerStateCommandMessage{key, result: Value|Void} (new ProtobufCodec.CreateGetEagerStateCommand), as transitions/journal.rs does, so journal layout is identical across attempts; in the Replaying branch, when the replayed entry originated from GetEagerStateCommand (already mapped at Protocol.cs:252), decode the embedded result from the stored command payload instead of treating the raw payload as the value. (4) Operations.cs SetState/ClearState/ClearAllState: perform the cache mutation in BOTH branches (move it above the Replaying early-return; SetState must still serialize during replay): set → _eagerState[key] = CopyToPooled(bytes); clear → _eagerState[key] = null (marker, NOT Remove, per EagerState::clear); clearAll → _eagerState.Clear() and _eagerStateIsPartial = false (per EagerState::clear_all).
findings.risks: Severity rationale: silent incorrect state reads (default instead of stored values) in any virtual-object/workflow handler that does SetState-then-Get under partial_state — which restate-server sends when eager state exceeds its size limit — plus journal mismatch on retry; the report's MEDIUM understates the silent-data-corruption mode, hence HIGH. Fix risks: emitting GetEagerStateCommandMessage changes the journal layout produced by this SDK (eager gets become journaled commands), so any in-flight invocations journaled by the old build would mismatch on replay after upgrade — acceptable pre-release but should be called out. The replay branch of GetStateAsync currently deserializes the raw replayed command payload as the user value (Operations.cs:477-478), which looks independently broken for both lazy (result arrives via completion notification) and eager (result is a protobuf field) replays — fixing B7's eager replay decode without auditing the lazy replay path may mask that adjacent bug. ClearState switching from Remove to a null marker requires the Get path to distinguish 'present-but-null' from 'absent', and CopyToPooled rentals for replay-time SetState slightly increase pooled memory held per invocation. GetStateKeysAsync has the analogous partial/eager gap (always lazy; Rust returns eager keys when complete) — out of scope for B7 but same data model enables it.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test InvocationStateMachine over in-memory duplex pipes. (1) Partial-state regression: StartMessage{partial_state:true, known_entries:1} + InputCommand; handler does SetState("a") then GetStateAsync("b"); assert the writer emitted GetLazyStateCommand for "b" (and the call awaits its completion notification) instead of returning default. (2) Complete-state: state_map={x:v}; Get("x")→v, Get("y")→default, and (post-fix) both emit GetEagerStateCommandMessage with Value/Void results. (3) Cleared marker: ClearState("x") then Get("x") → default with NO lazy command (complete and partial variants). (4) clear_all semantics: partial_state:true, ClearAllState(), Get("z") → default with no lazy command (is_partial flipped false). (5) Replay determinism: capture the full command stream from run (1), feed it back as known_entries with partial_state:true; assert identical handler results and zero command-mismatch — specifically that SetState during replay repopulates the cache so a post-replay Get("a") returns the set value without a lazy roundtrip. (6) Replay of GetEagerStateCommand entries decodes the embedded Void/Value result correctly. Cross-check expected wire sequences against sdk-shared-core's tests (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/tests/state.rs) for the same scenarios.

findings.title: Suspension never implemented: input-close parks handler forever, leaking handler task, DI scope, state machine, and pooled buffers

evidence.interpretation: When the runtime closes the input stream (the suspension trigger per shared-core), the incoming loop just breaks. It holds only incomingCts.Token (not the CTS), so it cannot cancel anything, completes silently, and neither faults pending TCSs nor emits a SuspensionMessage.

Every blocking operation (Call, Send, Sleep at line 603, awakeables, promises, attach) parks on a bare TCS await with no .WaitAsync(ct) or cancellation registration. Repo-wide grep finds zero WaitAsync usages in src/. Even RequestAborted (RST_STREAM after the runtime's abort-timeout) cannot unpark these awaits.
CompletionManager.CancelAll (the only mechanism that completes orphaned TCSs) and the ArrayPool returns run only in Dispose. Grep confirms CancelAll has no other caller in src/.

sm.Dispose() (which calls CancelAll) runs only when HandleAsync unwinds, but HandleAsync is blocked at line 62 on the handler which is blocked on a TCS that only Dispose can cancel — a circular wait. The comment's claim (b) is false: ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync never cancels incomingCts on connection close; incomingCts.Cancel is only called in the finally block (line 116), again downstream of the parked await.

evidence.quote:
await handler.HandleAsync(
    context.Request.BodyReader,
    context.Response.BodyWriter,
    service,
    handlerDef,
    context.RequestServices,
    context.RequestAborted);
...
await context.Response.CompleteAsync();
evidence.interpretation: The MapPost delegate awaits HandleAsync; Kestrel keeps the Http2Stream, the per-request DI scope (RequestServices), and the entire async continuation chain rooted until the delegate completes — which never happens while the handler is parked. The state machine, its rented pool arrays, the scoped services, and the parked handler task all leak until process restart.

evidence.quote: Suspension = 0x0001,
evidence.interpretation: The Suspension message type is declared but grep over src/ (excluding generated obj/) finds zero writers — the fork never sends SuspensionMessage at all.

Ground truth: in the Rust VM, when input is closed and the handler awaits uncompleted notifications (and no Run closure is executing), the VM hits the suspension point and returns Err(Suspended) (code 599) so the language SDK unwinds the parked handler instead of leaving it pending.

Ground truth: suspension sends a SuspensionMessage enumerating the awaited completion ids / signal ids, closes the output, and transitions to Closed — after which the SDK ends the HTTP response, releasing all per-invocation resources.
The service protocol mandates sending SuspensionMessage on suspension. The fork violates this MUST: it sends nothing and hangs.

findings.rootCause: The fork ported the shared-core journal/command plumbing but omitted the suspension state transition entirely. There is no equivalent of the Rust VM's input_is_closed check at await points: (1) ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync treats input-stream EOF as a no-op (break) instead of a suspension trigger; (2) all `await tcs.Task` sites are unlinked to any cancellation, so neither input-close nor RequestAborted can unpark the handler; (3) the only TCS cleanup (CompletionManager.CancelAll) lives in InvocationStateMachine.Dispose, which is reachable only after HandleAsync unwinds — i.e., only after the parked handler completes, a circular dependency. Because Kestrel roots the Http2Stream, the request DI scope, and the awaited task chain until the endpoint delegate returns, every suspension event (routine: any sleep/call/awakeable wait exceeding the runtime's inactivity timeout) permanently leaks the handler task, scoped services, the InvocationStateMachine with its rented ArrayPool buffers, and the HTTP/2 stream.
findings.correctBehavior: Per the shared-core VM (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/transitions/async_results.rs:147-157), when input is closed and the handler is awaiting notifications that are not yet completed — and no Run closure is currently executing — the VM executes HitSuspensionPoint (transitions/terminal.rs:20-53): it sends a SuspensionMessage listing the awaited completion ids / signal ids / named signals, sends EOF, transitions to State::Closed, and surfaces Err(Suspended) (error code 599, vm/errors.rs:74,119) to the awaiting handler so it unwinds immediately. protocol.proto:88-97 states "Implementations MUST send this message when suspending an invocation." The HTTP layer then completes the response and releases all per-invocation resources; the runtime later re-invokes with the journal replayed.
findings.fixSpec: Minimal port of the shared-core suspension transition: (1) CompletionManager: add `IReadOnlyCollection<int> PendingIndices` (slots with Kind==Tcs) and `void FailAll(Exception ex)` that TrySetException on all pending TCSs and clears. (2) Define `internal sealed class SuspendedException : Exception` (sentinel, mirrors Rust SUSPENDED 599). (3) InvocationStateMachine: add `WriteSuspensionAndCloseAsync()` that, when State==Processing and (_completions or _signalCompletions) have pending indices, writes a Suspension message (MessageType.Suspension, payload: SuspensionMessage proto with waiting_completions = pending completion ids, waiting_signals = pending signal ids), flushes, sets State=Closed, then calls FailAll(new SuspendedException()) on both managers. (4) Protocol.cs ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync: on `message is null` (input closed), invoke the suspension path from step 3 instead of bare break; if nothing is pending, leave behavior unchanged. (5) InvocationHandler.HandleAsync: add `catch (SuspendedException)` before the generic catch that logs suspension and returns WITHOUT calling FailAsync (the suspension frame already terminates the stream); the existing finally then disposes sm, returns pooled buffers, and the endpoint delegate completes, releasing the Kestrel stream and DI scope. (6) Defensive leak-stop for the abort path: in HandleAsync's finally is unreachable while parked, so additionally make the incoming task fault pending TCSs on ct-cancellation too (call FailAll(new OperationCanceledException(ct)) when ReadMessageAsync throws/returns due to abort) so RST_STREAM also unparks the handler. Caveat mirroring Rust's `any_executing` guard: do not suspend while a ctx.Run side-effect closure is still executing — if the fork tracks in-flight Run tasks, defer suspension until the Run command is flushed (at minimum document this; the Rust VM returns WaitingPendingRun in that case).
findings.risks: (1) Race between input-close and a handler that is mid-execution and has not yet registered its next TCS: the sweep-based fix only faults already-registered TCSs; a subsequent GetOrRegister+await after State==Closed must also observe suspension (EnsureActive already throws on Closed, but with InvalidOperationException — should be SuspendedException to avoid reporting a 500 ErrorMessage on a broken pipe). (2) Suspending while a ctx.Run closure is executing diverges from Rust's WaitingPendingRun guard and could lose a side-effect result that was about to be journaled; the runtime re-executes the Run on retry (at-least-once), which is protocol-acceptable but should be guarded like Rust does. (3) Writing the suspension frame races with the handler thread writing commands on the shared ProtocolWriter (single-writer assumption in the state machine); the suspension write happens on the incoming-reader task, so it needs the same synchronization the completion path already relies on — safest to only write after confirming the handler is parked (all pending slots are TCS). (4) FailAll with SuspendedException will surface through user code's try/catch(Exception) blocks; user handlers that swallow exceptions could mask suspension — same hazard exists in other SDKs (Java's AbortedExecutionException) and is acceptable. (5) Distinguish graceful input-close (suspend) from RequestAborted (abort, no suspension frame) to avoid writing to an aborted response.
findings.testStrategy: Unit tests with in-memory Pipe pairs driving InvocationHandler.HandleAsync directly: (1) Suspension happy path — send Start + InputCommand for a handler that calls ctx.Sleep (or Call), wait until the SleepCommand frame is flushed, then Complete() the request-side PipeWriter (input EOF) WITHOUT sending the completion notification; assert the response stream contains a Suspension frame (0x0001) whose waiting_completions equals the sleep's completion id, that HandleAsync completes within the test timeout, and that no ErrorMessage/EndMessage follows. (2) Leak regression — hold a WeakReference to the InvocationStateMachine (or scoped service) created during test (1); after HandleAsync returns, GC.Collect and assert the reference is dead; before the fix this test hangs (use a 5s timeout to demonstrate). (3) Abort path — cancel the outer CancellationToken while the handler is parked; assert HandleAsync returns promptly and pending TCSs are faulted/canceled (no hang). (4) Race test — deliver the completion notification and close the input in quick succession; assert the invocation completes normally with Output+End and no Suspension frame. (5) Cross-check expected frames against third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/tests/suspensions.rs scenarios for parity.

findings.title: CompletionManager register/complete TOCTOU races can silently drop a completion (lossy window is in TryComplete/TryFail, not the two spots the report cites)

evidence.interpretation: The window the report cites in Register exists exactly as described: between TryRemove (line 57) and the re-TryAdd (line 68), a concurrent TryComplete can insert a fresh Result slot, making the TryAdd fail silently (return value ignored). HOWEVER: (a) the returned tcs has ALREADY been resolved with the first completion before line 68, so the awaiting caller loses nothing — only a hypothetical second completion for the same index (which the protocol never sends within one stream) would be orphaned; (b) grep shows CompletionManager.Register is never called by production code — only by test/Restate.Sdk.Tests/Journal/CompletionManagerTests.cs. Production exclusively uses GetOrRegister (31 call sites in InvocationStateMachine.Operations.cs).

The report's second cited window also exists: the TryUpdate return value is ignored. But for it to 'lose against a concurrent TryComplete', a SECOND completion for the same entry index must arrive while the first is being consumed — the service protocol delivers exactly one notification per completion id per stream, so this cannot drop a real completion in production. If TryUpdate loses against a concurrent GetOrRegister instead, both callers receive TCSes already resolved with the same result — benign.

The genuinely lossy race the report does NOT name, in the same register/complete family: pump task calls TryComplete; TryRemove misses (handler not yet registered); handler task's GetOrRegister then atomically GetOrAdds a fresh unresolved TCS and starts awaiting it; pump's TryAdd at line 104 fails silently (return ignored, method still returns true) — the completion is dropped and the handler awaits forever until connection teardown (CancelAll). TryFail (lines 108-119) has the identical window. This needs only ONE completion message, so it is protocol-reachable.

Proves real concurrency: the message pump (which calls TryComplete/TryFail via HandleIncomingMessage in InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs lines 144-187) runs as a separate Task concurrently with the handler body (which calls GetOrRegister via Operations.cs). The CompletionManager comment (lines 43-45) acknowledges this two-task model.

For fresh commands the code deliberately registers before flushing, so the runtime cannot send a completion before the TCS exists — the TryComplete race window is closed for that path. But for REPLAYED entries and replayed awakeables (Awakeable() is purely local, no flush gate; an external party may resolve an awakeable created in a prior attempt at any time), the SignalNotification/CompletionNotification can arrive on the pump concurrently with the handler's GetOrRegister during replay — exactly the single-completion lossy interleaving.

Ground truth (v0.10.0): the shared-core VM stores notifications in plain non-concurrent collections mutated exclusively through &mut self — the VM is a sequential state machine, so notification arrival (notify_input/enqueue/insert_ready) and consumption (take_handle/take_notification) can never interleave. A notification arriving before registration is simply parked in `ready` and found later; one arriving after finds the waiter. The .NET port introduced a two-task concurrency model and therefore must make the register/complete handoff atomic to preserve the VM's lossless semantics.

findings.rootCause: CompletionManager performs non-atomic check-then-act sequences on a ConcurrentDictionary whose individual operations are atomic but whose compositions are not. Four windows exist: (1) Register lines 57-68 (TryRemove then ignored re-TryAdd), (2) GetOrRegister line 91 (ignored TryUpdate), (3) TryComplete lines 97-104 (failed TryRemove then ignored TryAdd), (4) TryFail lines 110-117 (same shape). The report's two cited windows (1) and (2) exist verbatim but cannot lose a real completion under the protocol's one-notification-per-completion-id guarantee — and Register is test-only dead code in production. The production-lossy window is (3)/(4): pump's TryComplete misses on TryRemove, handler's GetOrRegister atomically inserts a fresh TCS, pump's TryAdd fails silently, the completion is dropped, and the handler awaits the TCS until connection teardown. Reachable for replayed entries/awakeables where notification timing is not gated by the register-before-flush discipline used for fresh commands. The Rust shared-core VM (the reference) has no such race because AsyncResultsState is mutated only via &mut self in a sequential state machine; the .NET port added a concurrent pump task without making the register/complete handoff atomic.
findings.correctBehavior: Per third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/context.rs (v0.10.0) lines 147-300, all notification state lives in AsyncResultsState { to_process: VecDeque, ready: HashMap, handle_mapping: HashMap } with every method taking &mut self (enqueue line 180, insert_ready line 192, take_handle line 267, process_next_until_any_found line 207). The VM is a single-threaded state machine: a notification arriving before the SDK asks for it is parked in `ready` and atomically found later; a waiter registered first is atomically satisfied when the notification is processed. No interleaving can ever drop a notification — completion delivery is lossless by construction. The service protocol (dev/restate/service/protocol.proto) sends exactly one CompletionNotification/SignalNotification per completion_id/signal index per stream, so the SDK must never silently discard the single delivery.
findings.fixSpec: Serialize all CompletionManager state mutations under one lock, mirroring shared-core's exclusive-access model — contention is trivial (exactly two tasks per invocation-scoped instance). In src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/Journal/CompletionManager.cs: replace `ConcurrentDictionary<int, CompletionSlot> _slots` with `Dictionary<int, CompletionSlot> _slots` plus `private readonly object _gate = new();`, and wrap the bodies of Register, GetOrRegister, TryComplete, TryFail, and CancelAll in `lock (_gate)`. Inside the lock use plain TryGetValue/indexer assignment, eliminating all four TOCTOU windows: TryComplete/TryFail become 'if slot is Tcs -> TrySetResult/TrySetException else store Result/Failure' as one atomic step; GetOrRegister becomes 'if absent -> add Tcs and return it; if Tcs -> return it; if Result/Failure -> create resolved TCS, overwrite slot, return it'; Register keeps its duplicate-registration throw. Resolving the TCS inside the lock is safe because every TCS is created with TaskCreationOptions.RunContinuationsAsynchronously, so no continuation runs inline under the lock. Update the class-level comment (lines 43-45) to document the lock-based design. Optionally delete the production-unused Register(int) or leave it for the existing tests (minimal: leave it).
findings.risks: Very low. The lock is per-invocation with exactly two contending tasks (handler + pump), so no throughput impact. RunContinuationsAsynchronously guarantees TCS resolution under the lock cannot execute user continuations inline, avoiding deadlock/lock-hold inflation. CancelAll under the lock is fine for the same reason. Behavior-compatible: all 11 existing CompletionManagerTests semantics preserved (duplicate-Register throw, early-completion delivery, GetOrRegister idempotence, CancelAll idempotence). Main review point: ensure no new code path calls back into CompletionManager from a TCS continuation synchronously (none exists today).
findings.testStrategy: 1) Keep all existing tests in test/Restate.Sdk.Tests/Journal/CompletionManagerTests.cs green (dotnet test). 2) Add a race stress test reproducing the lossy window: loop ~10k iterations; each iteration creates a fresh CompletionManager and races Task.Run(() => mgr.TryComplete(0, result)) against Task.Run(() => mgr.GetOrRegister(0)); assert the returned tcs.Task completes with the result within a short timeout (e.g. 2s). Against the current code this hangs/fails intermittently (completion dropped when TryAdd at line 104 loses); under the lock it always passes. 3) Mirror the test for TryFail and for GetOrRegister racing a pre-stored early completion. 4) Optional integration check: replayed-awakeable scenario where a SignalNotification is fed to HandleIncomingMessage concurrently with Awakeable() registration, asserting the awakeable task resolves.

findings.title: Assorted: __restate_now name reuse, retry-exhaustion empty-success journal entry, non-durable retry elapsed, cached void future

evidence.interpretation: (a) Factually confirmed: every ctx.Now() journals a Run under the fixed name "__restate_now". However, no 'unique-name contract' exists — see proto/VM evidence below — so this is not a bug.

(a) Ground truth: RunCommand.name has no uniqueness requirement anywhere in the proto. In the shared-core VM (vm/mod.rs:1084-1095) sys_run stores the name only 'if enabled!(Level::DEBUG)' for logging; replay matching is positional (PopJournalEntry). A fixed name is deterministic across replays, so it is harmless and matches reference-SDK practice.

(b) Confirmed: the wire carries a failure proposal, but the in-memory journal records the entry as a completed (empty-SUCCESS) Run, and the TerminalException is thrown locally without awaiting the completion notification.
(b) The journal model has NO failure representation at all — a failed run cannot be recorded, only success bytes. This is why the exhaustion path appends an empty success.

(b) On replay, the Run branch returns the raw replayed RunCommand payload and never consumes the RunCompletionNotificationMessage; a journaled run failure (the runtime stores the proposed failure) can therefore never re-raise TerminalException on replay — compensation branches taken live diverge on replay. The report is right about the symptom, but the cause is the replay/notification path (overlaps B1), not the local empty-success append per se (the in-memory journal is discarded between attempts).
