(b) Ground truth: a run's terminal failure is durably stored and delivered (live and on replay) as a notification carrying the failure oneof; the SDK must surface that failure as a TerminalException in both cases. shared-core vm/transitions/journal.rs:760-776 maps NextRetry::DoNotRetry to Result::Failure and the SDK then awaits the notification, guaranteeing deterministic re-raise.

(c) Confirmed factually: elapsed is process-local wall clock; the SDK ignores StartMessage.retry_count_since_last_stored_entry (field 7) and duration_since_last_stored_entry (field 8) which shared-core uses in infer_entry_retry_info (context.rs:461-480). However the proto itself says those fields 'might not be accurate, as it's not durably stored' — so even ground truth retry accounting is best-effort. Non-bug, as the report concedes ('fine live').

(d) Confirmed safe: the cached instance is immutable (pre-completed TCS, InvocationId => null), so sharing is harmless; GetResult()=>true is a deliberate IDurableFuture<bool> design for void ops. Additionally — beyond the report — grep shows Completed()/CachedCompleted/CreateCompleted() have ZERO callers (the only construction in product code is `new VoidDurableFuture(task.Result)` at DefaultContext.cs:92): it is dead code.

findings.rootCause: Four independent observations, all factually present but only one a real defect: (a) ctx.Now() reuses the fixed run name "__restate_now" — true, but neither protocol.proto (RunCommandMessage.name, line 507) nor the shared-core VM (sys_run keeps names only for DEBUG logging; replay matching is positional) imposes a unique-name contract, so this is a non-bug. (b) The retry-exhaustion path proposes a run FAILURE on the wire but the SDK's journal model (JournalEntry) cannot represent failures, so it appends an empty-SUCCESS Run entry and throws TerminalException locally; combined with the Run replay path (Protocol.cs ReplayNextEntryAsync) that returns the raw RunCommand payload and never consumes RunCompletionNotificationMessage, a journaled run failure is never re-raised as TerminalException on replay — live and replay executions diverge (saga compensation branches). This is the genuine defect, and its root is the missing failure channel in journal+replay (shared root with B1), not the local append alone. (c) Retry elapsed uses process-local DateTimeOffset.UtcNow and ignores StartMessage fields 7/8 — non-durable, but the proto declares those fields best-effort anyway; non-bug. (d) VoidDurableFuture.Completed() returns a shared immutable pre-completed instance — thread-safe; GetResult()=>true is cosmetic; the method is in fact dead code (no callers).
findings.correctBehavior: Per protocol.proto: ProposeRunCompletionMessage "won't be written to the journal immediately, but will appear later as a new notification (meaning the result was stored)" (lines 134-137), and RunCompletionNotificationMessage carries `oneof result { Value value = 5; Failure failure = 6; }` (lines 512-522). Per the shared-core VM (vm/transitions/journal.rs, ProposeRunCompletion transition): on retry exhaustion (NextRetry::DoNotRetry) the VM sends Result::Failure and the SDK then awaits the run's completion notification; on replay the same failure notification is replayed, so a terminal run failure deterministically re-raises in every attempt. Run names carry no uniqueness requirement (proto line 507; vm/mod.rs:1084-1095 stores names only for debug logs). Retry accounting in shared-core combines runtime-provided StartInfo (context.rs infer_entry_retry_info, lines 461-480) with SDK-measured attempt_duration, and the proto explicitly notes these counters are not durably stored.
findings.fixSpec: Only (b) needs a code fix; (d) warrants dead-code removal; (a) and (c) need no change. For (b), minimal fix aligned with shared-core semantics: (1) Add a failure representation to JournalEntry (e.g. `static JournalEntry Failed(JournalEntryType type, ushort code, string message, string? name)` with an IsFailure flag or stored Failure payload) in src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/Journal/JournalEntry.cs. (2) In both ExecuteWithRetryAsync exhaustion paths (InvocationStateMachine.Operations.cs ~128-141 and ~174-184), append that failed entry instead of `JournalEntry.Completed(Run, empty)`. (3) In the Run replay branches (RunSyncReplayAsync, RunAsync/RunFutureAsync replaying paths), after replaying the RunCommand, consume the matching RunCompletionNotificationMessage (by result_completion_id) and throw TerminalException(failure.code, failure.message) when the failure oneof is set — this is the piece that actually restores deterministic re-raise; it should be implemented jointly with the B1 fix to the Run replay/notification path so notifications are not double-handled. For (d), delete the unused CachedCompleted, Completed() and CreateCompleted() members (src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/DurableFuture.cs lines 66, 92-103).
findings.risks: The (b) fix touches the shared Run replay path: ReplayNextEntryAsync currently stops at the next command and treats the raw command payload as the result for ALL entry types, so adding notification consumption for Run must not disturb replay of other entry kinds or notification ordering/index accounting (completion ids are derived from _journal.Count). It overlaps B1 — fixing both independently risks double-consuming RunCompletionNotification messages; coordinate into one change. Adding a failure field to the readonly JournalEntry struct changes a hot-path value type (size/copies). Dead-code removal of VoidDurableFuture.Completed() is zero-risk (no callers, internal). Do NOT 'fix' (a) by uniquifying names: a non-deterministic suffix would break shared-core-style command equality checks; the fixed name is correct.
findings.testStrategy: Protocol-level integration tests against the state machine with in-memory duplex pipes: (1) Live exhaustion: ctx.Run with a RetryPolicy(maxAttempts=1) and an always-throwing action — assert the wire contains RunCommand followed by ProposeRunCompletionMessage with the Failure oneof, and the handler observes TerminalException. (2) Replay determinism (the key regression test for (b)): feed StartMessage(known_entries=3) + InputCommand + replayed RunCommand + RunCompletionNotificationMessage{failure} — assert ctx.Run rethrows TerminalException with the same code/message so a try/catch compensation branch executes identically to the live attempt; today this test fails (replay returns/deserializes raw command bytes instead). (3) Name reuse (a): call ctx.Now() twice, capture two RunCommands both named "__restate_now", then replay both entries and assert deterministic results — proves no uniqueness requirement is violated. (4) (d): remove the dead members and verify the solution builds and existing Timer/combinator tests pass (VoidDurableFuture instances are still constructed via `new` in DefaultContext.Timer). For (c), no behavioral assertion change — optionally document non-durable retry budgets in XML docs.

architecture.summary: The fork's core replay/concurrency model diverges structurally from sdk-shared-core v0.10.0 in three compounding ways, all CONFIRMED (confidence 0.95): (B1) it conflates wire completion-ids with journal array indices (completionId = _journal.Count) and during replay never pairs notifications at all — it deserializes the replayed COMMAND's protobuf payload as the user result; (B2) IsReplaying compares journal count against StartMessage.known_entries, but the proto explicitly states known_entries is "the sum of known commands + notifications", so any completed entry in the replayed journal leaves the SDK permanently 'replaying' and reading the pipe for commands that will never come; (B3) ReplayNextEntryAsync reads the PipeReader concurrently with ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync (two readers on one ProtocolReader), racing and silently dropping replayed commands (HandleIncomingMessage ignores command-typed messages). The Rust VM proves all three must be fixed as ONE redesign: buffer all known_entries messages up front (commands into a queue, notifications into an async-results table keyed by NotificationId), end replay when the command queue empties, and allocate completion ids from a dedicated counter starting at 1 (signals from 17).
architecture.currentFlow: START: InvocationHandler.HandleAsync (src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/InvocationHandler.cs:44) awaits sm.StartAsync, THEN spawns the pump at line 50 (incomingTask = sm.ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync). StartAsync (src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/StateMachine/InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs:16-102) reads StartMessage + InputCommand from _reader, then loops `for (var i = 1; i < (int)fields.KnownEntries; i++)` (line 67) reading known_entries-1 more messages: commands are appended as `JournalEntry.Completed(MapMessageTypeToEntry(type), detachedMem)` with the RAW COMMAND PAYLOAD stored as the entry Result (lines 74-79); notifications go to HandleIncomingMessage (line 83). REPLAY-END TEST: InvocationJournal.IsReplaying => `Count < KnownEntries` (src/Restate.Sdk/Internal/Journal/InvocationJournal.cs:20); Protocol.cs:90 only transitions to Processing if !_journal.IsReplaying. PUMP: ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync (Protocol.cs:104-121) loops _reader.ReadMessageAsync; HandleIncomingMessage (Protocol.cs:123-192) handles EntryAck/SignalNotification/IsNotification — command messages fall through and are SILENTLY DROPPED. Notification pairing: `var entryIndex = (int)notification.CompletionId;` (Protocol.cs:166) — wire completion_id used directly as the CompletionManager key, which elsewhere is the journal index. PER-OP REPLAY: every operation in InvocationStateMachine.Operations.cs branches on `State == InvocationState.Replaying` and calls ReplayNextEntryAsync (Protocol.cs:200-234) which calls `_reader.ReadMessageAsync` AGAIN (line 207) — a SECOND CONCURRENT READER racing the pump — looping until a command appends to the journal, then returns the entry whose Result is the command's protobuf bytes; callers do `Deserialize<TResponse>(replay.Result)` (e.g. CallAsync at Operations.cs:274, RunAsync at :58-59, GetPromiseAsync at :683-684) — i.e. they JSON-deserialize protobuf-encoded CallCommandMessage/RunCommandMessage bytes as the user value. PER-OP PROCESSING: completion id is allocated as `var completionId = (uint)_journal.Count` (Operations.cs:197/285/362/390/415/443/481/563/592/687/713/739/757/785/880), TCS registered in _completions keyed by journal index (Operations.cs:288 etc.). Calls hack around needing TWO ids by appending a dummy completed journal entry ("BUG 1 FIX: Allocate dummy slot", Operations.cs:280-283, 357-360, 780-783, 875-878) — but the REPLAY path for Call appends only ONE entry per CallCommand (Protocol.cs:212-215), so journal counts diverge between first execution and replay, and post-replay completion ids collide with ids already consumed in the replayed prefix. Signals: `_nextSignalIndex` starts at 0 (InvocationStateMachine.cs:31), _signalCompletions keyed by raw idx (Protocol.cs:139-151). Pending Awakeable/Sleep across suspension: no SuspensionMessage support exists anywhere in src/ (only the enum value MessageType.Suspension = 0x0001).
architecture.correctFlow: The Rust CoreVM (third_party/sdk-shared-core/src/vm/) is single-threaded with ONE input feed: notify_input pushes bytes into a Decoder and every decoded frame goes through do_transition(NewMessage) (mod.rs:296-315) — there is no second reader, ever. PREFLIGHT: protocol.proto:60-61 documents `known_entries = 3` as "This is the sum of known commands + notifications". State::WaitingReplayEntries { received_entries, commands: VecDeque<RawMessage>, async_results } (mod.rs:46-50): each incoming message increments received_entries regardless of kind (transitions/input.rs:120-148 PostReceiveEntry); commands are pushed to the commands VecDeque (input.rs:110-112), notifications are decoded and enqueued into AsyncResultsState.to_process (input.rs:86-89). When received_entries == entries_to_replay → State::Replaying. REPLAY END: replay ends when the buffered COMMAND QUEUE empties — PopJournalEntry (transitions/journal.rs:817-852): `let new_state = if commands.is_empty() { State::Processing { processing_first_entry: true, .. } } else { State::Replaying {..} }`. Replay NEVER touches input; each Sys* op pops the next command, decodes it, and runs check_entry_header_match for non-determinism detection (journal.rs:887-902). COMPLETION IDS: a dedicated counter completion_index starting at 1 ("Clever trick for protobuf here", context.rs:101-113), advanced by next_completion_notification_id() per completable result — sys_call consumes TWO (invocation_id + result, mod.rs:742-744); sys_send consumes ONE; signal_index starts at 17 because "1 to 16 are reserved!" (context.rs:108-109; cancel = SignalId(1)). These counters advance IDENTICALLY during replay and processing, so ids are deterministic across attempts. ASYNC-RESULTS TABLE: AsyncResultsState (context.rs:146-288) = to_process: VecDeque<Notification> + ready: HashMap<NotificationId, NotificationResult> + handle_mapping: NotificationHandle -> NotificationId, where NotificationId = CompletionId(u32) | SignalId(u32) | SignalName(String). Awaiting (DoProgress, transitions/async_results.rs:15-165): check ready, then drain to_process until any awaited id found; in Replaying a miss is a hard non-determinism error (lines 50-112 — proof: if an await can't complete during replay there can be no commands left); in Processing a miss yields ExecuteRun (pending ctx.run closures), ReadFromInput, or — if input_is_closed and nothing is executing — HitSuspensionPoint emitting SuspensionMessage with waiting_completions/waiting_signals (transitions/terminal.rs:18-40, proto:88-97). Replayed VALUES come from notifications looked up by the command's result_completion_id, never from command payloads; GetEagerStateCommand is the one exception where the value is embedded in the command and converted into a synthetic ready Notification keyed by the freshly allocated completion id (journal.rs:411-437). Run during replay: if a notification for the run's completion id exists in ready or to_process (non_deterministic_find_id), the closure is NOT executed; otherwise it is enqueued for execution (journal.rs:682-712).
architecture.unifiedRedesign: One coherent change, mirroring CoreVM inside the existing partial-class layout — the three bugs cannot be fixed independently because B2's wrong predicate is what forces B3's second pipe-reader, and B3's command-dropping pump is only survivable because B1 never looks at notification ids during replay. DESIGN: (1) SINGLE READER + PREFLIGHT BUFFERING (B3): StartAsync reads exactly known_entries messages (Input is entry #1) and never lets anything else touch _reader: each command frame is parsed just enough to extract {MessageType, name, result_completion_id(s), embedded eager result} and pushed into a new `Queue<ReplayCommand>` owned by InvocationJournal; each notification frame is routed immediately into CompletionManager (completion ids) / signal completions (idx or name), which already buffers early results. After StartAsync, ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync is the ONLY pipe reader; ReplayNextEntryAsync stops reading the pipe entirely and becomes a synchronous DequeueReplayCommand(expectedType, name) that pops the queue, verifies type/name (non-determinism check per Rust check_entry_header_match), and flips State to Processing when the queue empties. A command frame arriving after preflight becomes a ProtocolException instead of being silently dropped. (2) ID ALLOCATION + PAIRING (B1): add dedicated counters on InvocationStateMachine: `_commandIndex` (debug/error metadata), `_completionIndex = 1`, `_signalIndex = 17` (replacing _nextSignalIndex=0). Every completable op allocates `NextCompletionId()` in BOTH the processing AND replay paths (Call allocates two: invocationIdNotificationIdx then resultCompletionId, exactly like sys_call) — this makes ids deterministic across attempts and deletes the dummy-journal-slot hack and every `completionId = (uint)_journal.Count`. CompletionManager keys become wire completion ids, never journal indices. Replay of a completable command awaits `_completions.GetOrRegister((int)resultCompletionId)` — the value comes from the buffered (or still-in-flight, completed later by the pump) notification, never from command payload bytes; replay of GetEagerStateCommand synthesizes an immediate completion from the embedded result; replay of Run checks whether a completion for its id is already buffered/ready — if yes, await it without executing the closure; if no, execute the closure and propose the completion (Rust SysRun semantics). Non-completable commands (SetState/ClearState/CompleteAwakeable/SendSignal/Output) just dequeue+verify. (3) REPLAY PREDICATE (B2): IsReplaying => replay command queue non-empty (commands-only), with `processing_first_entry`-equivalent transition when it drains; KnownEntries is used only to count preflight messages. InvocationState becomes derived: WaitingStart → (StartAsync buffers known_entries msgs) → Replaying iff queue non-empty after Input → Processing. The journal's appended-entry array shrinks to a command log (for command_index and debugging), no longer the id source. This also unlocks correct suspension later: when input closes and awaited completion ids are pending, emit SuspensionMessage(waiting_completions=[ids]) — the data needed (the awaited wire ids) only exists once pairing is by completion id.

editPlan.change: Add `ReplayCommand` struct: {MessageType Type, string? Name, uint[] CompletionIds, ReadOnlyMemory<byte> EagerResult, bool HasEagerResult, ReadOnlyMemory<byte> RawPayload}. Keep JournalEntry only as the live command log record (drop Pending/WithCompletion result-carrying semantics once nothing reads entry.Result as a user value).

Add ParseReplayCommand(MessageType, ReadOnlySpan<byte>) extracting result_completion_id (field 11 on completable commands), invocation_id_notification_idx (CallCommand field 10 / OneWayCallCommand), name, and the embedded result oneof for GetEagerStateCommand/GetEagerStateKeysCommand (convert keys to JSON string[] reusing the BUG-4 StateKeys logic). Add SignalName routing to ParseSignalNotification consumers.

Replace `IsReplaying => Count < KnownEntries` with `IsReplaying => _replayCommands.Count > 0`; add Queue<ReplayCommand> + EnqueueReplay/TryDequeueReplay; keep Count/Append purely as the live command index. This is the B2 fix and the pivot the rest hangs on.

No structural change (early-buffering GetOrRegister/TryComplete already correct) but document/enforce that keys are WIRE completion ids; optionally add a string-keyed overlay for named signals. Remove the Register() duplicate-throw path if unused after redesign.

Add `_completionIndex = 1`, `_signalIndex = 17` counters with NextCompletionId()/NextSignalId(); delete _nextSignalIndex; Initialize() no longer flips State by knownEntries>0 alone — Replaying iff replay queue non-empty after Input is consumed (knownEntries==0 should be a protocol error per Rust KNOWN_ENTRIES_IS_ZERO).

DELETE the pipe-read loop in ReplayNextEntryAsync (lines 200-234); replace with synchronous DequeueReplayCommand(expectedType, name) doing type/name verification + Processing transition on queue-empty. ProcessIncomingMessagesAsync is now provably the sole reader (B3 fix).

editPlan.change: No behavioral change required (pump already starts after StartAsync); optionally simplify the cancel/await-incoming teardown since replay no longer races the pump; later, suspension support hooks in here (translate a Suspended signal from the SM into clean stream close).

Audit only: they consume TaskCompletionSource<CompletionResult> opaquely, so they survive the redesign untouched; verify CallFuture replay path no longer fabricates `idx = _journal.Count - 1` (Operations.cs:348) since that lookup key changes meaning.

architecture.conflictGroups: GROUP A (must be sequenced AFTER and rebased onto the B1+B2+B3 core redesign — same files, same lines): anything touching InvocationStateMachine.Operations.cs, InvocationStateMachine.Protocol.cs, InvocationStateMachine.cs, ProtobufCodec.cs, InvocationJournal.cs, CompletionManager.cs. Concretely from the in-code "BUG n FIX" markers and observed divergences this covers: the existing BUG-1 dummy-slot hack (Operations.cs:280/357/780/875 — DELETED by the redesign, any fix-of-the-fix conflicts), BUG-4 StateKeys notification parsing (ProtobufCodec.cs:107 — reused by ParseReplayCommand for GetEagerStateKeys), the signal-index-base bug (_nextSignalIndex starts at 0 vs Rust's reserved 1-16/start-17 — absorbed into the new _signalIndex counter in InvocationStateMachine.cs), cancel-signal (SignalId 1) handling in HandleIncomingMessage, named-signal (SignalName) routing, Run-replay-executes-closure semantics, eager-state GetStateAsync bypass (Operations.cs:468-473 short-circuits without emitting GetEagerStateCommand or consuming a completion id — silently breaks command_index/completion_index determinism, MUST be fixed inside the Operations.cs rewrite, not separately), SendAsync blocking on the invocation-id notification, and suspension/SuspensionMessage support (needs the completion-id pairing to know waiting_completions). GROUP B (independent files, can proceed in parallel with the core redesign): OutputCommand void/value oneof (BUG-2 marker, already done in ProtobufCodec.CreateOutputCommand — only superficial overlap), ErrorMessage enrichment (related_command_index/stacktrace fields), ProtocolWriter/MessageHeader/MessageFlags framing fixes, ProtocolReader AdvanceTo/backpressure fixes (ProtocolReader.cs — note its examined-position comment at lines 44-54 was written to tolerate the dual-reader replay pattern; re-validate after B3 but the file itself doesn't conflict), discovery/EndpointManifest, Serde, Context surface (DefaultContext and siblings), InvocationHandler error mapping, and anything under samples/ or tests. GROUP C (semi-independent but verify after): DurableFuture.cs combinators (ctx.All/Race) — no code conflict, but their Task identities come from CompletionManager whose keying changes; re-run their tests after Group A lands.

Confirm the canonical B4-B10 list against GROUP A/B assignments before sequencing.

known_entries == 0: Rust hard-errors (KNOWN_ENTRIES_IS_ZERO, input.rs:65-67) because Input is always entry 0; .NET Initialize() accepts 0 and jumps to Processing (InvocationStateMachine.cs:126). Should the .NET fail the invocation with PROTOCOL_VIOLATION (571) for parity, or is leniency intentional for tests?

Run during replay when the completion was never journaled: Rust enqueues the closure for execution via RunState and DoProgress returns ExecuteRun. The .NET has no DoProgress pump on the user thread — proposal is to execute the closure inline in the replay branch when no buffered completion exists. Confirm this is acceptable (it changes execution thread/timing vs Rust but preserves semantics).

Eager state (_initialState) currently short-circuits GetStateAsync without writing GetEagerStateCommand or consuming a completion id; fixing it (required for deterministic completion_index) changes the wire output of existing deployments mid-journal. Is a compatibility shim needed for journals recorded by the buggy build, or is this fork pre-release with no journal-compat obligation?

Suspension: the redesign makes SuspensionMessage implementable (awaited wire completion ids are finally known), but implementing it requires deciding when .NET 'input is closed' (pump observes ReadMessageAsync == null) should suspend vs fail — should that land inside this redesign or as the immediately-following change?

ProtocolReader.ReadMessageAsync's AdvanceTo(buffer.Start) examined-position strategy (ProtocolReader.cs:42-54) was tuned for the dual-reader replay pattern; after B3, verify the pump cannot spin hot when partial frames sit in the buffer (examined should likely be buffer.End on the not-enough-data path only, which it already is — needs a unit test).

SendAsync currently blocks awaiting the invocation-id notification before returning InvocationHandle (Operations.cs:325-327); Rust returns a handle immediately and resolves lazily. Keeping the blocking behavior is safe but serializes sends — decide whether to fix in this pass (same file) or accept the latency.
